mainEntity.name: Do I need an expensive long lens for garden wildlife?

acceptedAnswer.text: Almost certainly not for UK gardens. A 70-200mm zoom positioned 6 metres from a thoughtfully-placed feeder produces frames that would require a 500mm in a wild setting. The 100-400mm zoom is the most versatile single-lens option. Save the super-telephoto budget for travel, tripod, mentoring, or printing.

mainEntity.name: What's the best food to attract birds for photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Sunflower hearts attract the widest range of small UK garden birds. Mealworms bring in robins, blackbirds, song thrushes. Suet cakes attract starlings and woodpeckers. Niger seed for goldfinches. Avoid mixed seed with too much filler. Clean feeders weekly to prevent disease.

mainEntity.name: Can I photograph birds through a kitchen window?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — clean glass both sides, shoot perpendicular to the glass, turn off interior lights. A black cloth behind shooting position helps. Tape a cardboard surround around the lens against glass to block reflected light. Most successful in winter.

mainEntity.name: How long until birds start using my feeder?

acceptedAnswer.text: 1-3 days if neighbouring gardens have established feeders. 2-4 weeks if starting from scratch. Don't move the feeder. Keep food topped up consistently — intermittent feeding teaches birds to look elsewhere.

mainEntity.name: What flowers attract the most pollinators for photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Lavender (July peak), buddleia (late July-August), verbena bonariensis, echinacea, hebes, heleniums, sedum. Native plants outperform showy hybrids — many double-flowered varieties produce no nectar. Plant in groups of 3-5. Avoid neonicotinoid-treated bedding.

mainEntity.name: How do I photograph hedgehogs at night?

acceptedAnswer.text: Confirm activity via trail camera first. Options: off-camera flash positioned to one side; high-ISO available-light at dusk; permanent low-power LED that hedgehogs habituate to. Always prioritise welfare — long lenses, minimal disturbance, never bait beyond natural attraction.

mainEntity.name: Should I use a hide in my own garden?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes for nervous species and behavioural shots. £100-200 pop-up hide transforms what's photographable — sparrowhawks, jays, woodpeckers become realistic. Regular feeder visitors tolerate stationary photographers. Test without hide first; if species fly off when you raise camera, hide pays for itself.

mainEntity.name: Is bird photography ethical when feeding bait stations?

acceptedAnswer.text: Garden feeding is broadly ethical and beneficial — feeders provide significant winter calories and support populations. Keep feeders clean (trichomonosis prevention). Appropriate food only. Position away from cat ambush points. Don't suddenly stop feeding — taper gradually if discontinuing.
